title: The Laundry Quarter Paradox: A Birthday Problem Estimation
text: Have you ever wondered if the quarter you just dropped into the laundry machine is one you’ve held before? A friend recently asked me this very question. It sounds like a needle-in-a-haystack scenario, but when we look at it through the lens of the Generalized Birthday Problem, the odds might be higher than you’d expect.

title: The Laundry Quarter Paradox: A Birthday Problem Estimation
text:
The Math
To estimate this, we consider: - \(n\): The total number of quarters you’ve handled over a period of time. - \(N\): The total pool of quarters in circulation in your local area (e.g., your metro area).
The probability \(P\) that at least two quarters in your sample of \(n\) are the same is given by:
\[P(n, N) \approx 1 - e^{-\frac{n(n-1)}{2N}}\]
For small values of \(n^2/2N\), we can use the Taylor series approximation:
\[P(n, N) \approx \frac{n^2}{2N}\]

title: The Laundry Quarter Paradox: A Birthday Problem Estimation
text:
Interactive Estimation
Adjust the sliders below to see how your habits and your city’s size affect the likelihood of a “quarter collision.”



Code
viewof quartersPerWeek = Inputs.range([1, 100], {value: 20, step: 1, label: "Quarters spent/week"})
viewof years = Inputs.range([0.1, 50], {value: 3, step: 0.1, label: "Years in area"})
viewof metroPopulation = Inputs.number({value: 1000000, label: "Metro Population (Pool size $N$)"})



























Code
n = quartersPerWeek * 52 * years
prob = 1 - Math.exp(-(n * (n - 1)) / (2 * metroPopulation))
taylorProb = (n * n) / (2 * metroPopulation)

md`
### Results

Over **${years}** years spending **${quartersPerWeek}** quarters a week, you've handled approximately **${d3.format(",")(Math.round(n))}** quarters.

In a pool of **${d3.format(",")(metroPopulation)}** quarters, the probability of having used the same quarter twice is:

## ${d3.format(".2%")(prob)}

*(Taylor Series Approximation: ${d3.format(".4%")(taylorProb)})*
`


































Assumptions
This model assumes a “perfectly mixed” pool where every quarter in the metro area has an equal chance of landing in your pocket. In reality, quarters might cluster in local businesses or laundromats, potentially increasing the local probability even further!
What do you think? Does this make you want to start marking your quarters?
